items.text: 「フジロック フェスティバル’26（FUJI ROCK FESTIVAL'26）」の開幕まで、あと2日。登場するアーティストのファッションにも毎年注目が集まりますが、昨年のフジロックで最も服装が話題になったミュージシャンの1人として挙げられるのは、「カトパコ」こと「カトリエル&パコ・アモロソ（CA7RIEL & Paco Amoroso）」ではないでしょうか。 彼らがステージで着用したのは、「アンリアレイジ（ANREALAGE）」が2025年春夏コレクションで発表した「空調服」*とのコラボレーションアイテムです。 *「空調服」は株式会社空調服のファン付きウェア、その附属品、及びこれらを示すブランド これまで空調ウェアが着用されていたのは、建設業や運送業などのタフなワークシーンがほとんどでしたが、近年続く記録的な猛暑により一般層への認知が飛躍的に拡大。ワークウェアメーカーだけでなく、アウトドアブランドやデザイナーズブランドなどが参入したことで、デザイン性が高いアイテムが数多く登場しています。 今年6月に開催された2027年春夏のパリメンズファッションウィークで大きな話題となったのが、「リック・オウエンス（Rick Owens）」が発表した空調ウェアです。協業した「アディダス（adidas）」が独自開発した空調システムを搭載しているそう。リック・オウエンスの前衛的なシルエットと、アディダスのテクノロジーが融合したインパクト満点の一着ですが、多くのミュージシャンに愛される両ブランドのコラボだけに、来年のフジロックのステージではこのアヴァンギャルドな空調ウェアを着用してパフォーマンスを行うミュージシャンが見られるかもしれませんね。 今後、さらに日常に溶け込んでいくことが予想される空調ウェア。野外フェスでも活躍しそうな、おしゃれな空調ウェアの注目4ブランドの新作をピックアップしました。 このコンテンツは FASHIONSNAP が配信しています。
items.title: 「カトパコ」着用で注目 野外フェスに着ていきたい最新デザイン空調ウェア4選

items.text: ​Constructed Views: Coyote Urbanism Presenting a critical urban analysis of Los Angeles through the lived experience of a coyote, Sukanya Mukherjee and Arnar Skarphedinsson explore the commodification of architecture in the city for the animated short Coyote Urbanism . Working between Los Angeles and Reykjavik, the Icelandic-Indian designer-director duo observe the impact of neoliberalization as the recognizable landscapes of Los Angeles give way to unfamiliar machines. When a solitary coyote's home becomes the next frontier of development, he wanders the streets and highways for a new place to rest, encountering the disparity and spectacle that shapes the urban environment, competing for dominance and leaving him little room to exist. Illustrating the urbanism of Los Angeles as a city formless in nature, instead tied together through individual experience and identity, Coyote Urbanism reframes the storied history of a resident population obscured in the city’s pursuit of “progress” – as the coyote is pushed to its edges by the infrastructure of machines taking over the urban fabric.
items.title: ​Constructed Views: Coyote Urbanism

items.text: ​In Motion: Doug Aitken’s Lightscape - Factory Tracing the search for individuality in a world of continuous rapid transformation, American artist Doug Aitken reveals a landscape of diversity on a cinematic journey through the U.S. for Lightscape – a new film-based installation on view at The Shed in New York City. Taken from the hour-long feature, Doug Aitken’s Lightscape - Factory observes factory workers against their automated, industrialized environment, entering into a rhythmic, kinetic synergy with the machines around them – featuring L.A. Dance Project’s Daphne Fernberger and Krump dancer Kevin “Konkrete” Davis, alongside actor-dancer Nathan Mitchell. A vulnerable, humanistic portrait that considers the hyper-connectivity of modern life, the film captures the blurring of humanity, technology and the environment, where bodies become a direct reflection of their mechanized surroundings through driving, twitching choreography. Led by a unique musical collaboration with the LA Master Chorale and the LA Phil, alongside sound design by Dražen Bošnjak, the visual and sonic landscapes shift gears as each character’s narrative evolves, guiding a state of transformation through haunting vocals and hardcore techno. Doug Aitken’s Lightscape is on view at The Shed in New York City until 13 September.
items.title: ​In Motion: Doug Aitken’s Lightscape - Factory

items.text: ​Raw Materials: Frank Bowling Born in British Guiana – now Guyana – celebrated London-based artist Frank Bowling has spent over six decades expanding the possibilities of paint. Defining painting as a way of thinking rather than explaining, his work reflects on intuitive color and the distinct quality of Guyanese light, utilizing gravity and chance for poured paintings that build layers of color into heavily textured canvases. Since graduating from the Royal College of Art in 1962, Bowling has developed an ambitious practice that closely engages with the materiality of his medium, continuing to paint daily while adapting to working from a wheelchair. Talking to friend and art critic Martin Gayford, he opens conversation about memory, process and perception for the short film Frank Bowling: Seeking the Sublime – directed by Lucy Andia and Frederick Shelbourne for the Fitzwilliam Museum in Cambridge. - Lucy Andia & Frederick Shelbourne At 92, the documentary meets Bowling in a moment of late-career clarity, observing his fluid evolution into abstraction, and a professional life that traversed the Atlantic several times over, between London and New York. Coinciding with the retrospective exhibition of the same name, Frank Bowling: Seeking the Sublime connects the artist’s archives with new footage captured at his London studio, documenting the shifting process and perspective of one of the foremost artists of his generation.
items.title: ​Raw Materials: Frank Bowling

items.text: ​They Call Me… Colette Infamous visual artist Colette was a cornerstone of the 70s and 80s New York art scene that immortalized Jean-Michel Basquiat, Keith Haring, and Andy Warhol, as one of the only prominent female artists of the era. Recognizing the obstacles imposed by her gender, and testing the theory that true fame and success as an artist can only be reached posthumously, Colette began an ongoing process of staging her own death – emerging as a new artistic persona with each demise, becoming Justine, then House of Olympia, and Countess Reichenbach. - Nick Hampson Shot between New York City and Maine, director Nick Hampson traces the many faces of the Tunisian-born artist for the short film Colette: Beautiful Dreamer , capturing the versatility and freedom afforded by her choice to be unbound to a single identity. Returning to her seminal installation and performance works, where reality intersects with staged environments – built from the confines of her Manhattan apartment – the documentary observes an evolving artist defined by her multiplicitous nature and punk sensibility, marking a moment of change through ritual as the artist synchronizes her past selves in a new, final form.
items.title: ​They Call Me… Colette

items.text: ​Surf’s Up: Water, Get Enemy? Against the shoreline of Tarkwa Bay and the waters surrounding Lagos, photographer and filmmaker Daniel Obasi follows the young Nigerian surfers whose lives have become entwined with the ocean for Water, Get Enemy?. Through a series of intimate interviews and reflections, the short film explores surfing as more than just a sport, presenting a means of survival, self-expression, and freedom from the vibrant capital’s southernmost point. - Daniel Obasi Connecting with the city’s surfers through their experiences growing up around the water, Nigerian-born Obasi reveals a tight group united by years of friendship, collective struggles, and a passion for the sea. Surfing together since childhood, the community functions like a family, building shared stories that began on the beach and in the water, and friendships that extend beyond surfing itself. Capturing the Atlantic ocean as a place of healing, identity, and community, Water, Get Enemy? observes a rarely seen side of Lagos, where young Nigerians carve out spaces of joy and discovery along the coastline, as the surfers of Tarkwa Bay find belonging, liberation and self-expression riding the waves.
items.title: ​Surf’s Up: Water, Get Enemy?

items.text: ​LoveSick: Chambers Street Processing the ghost of a former partner, writer and director Scott D. Keenan captures the resonance of romantic grief through movement for the short film Chambers Street . Choreographed by movement artist Max Cookward, and featuring dancers Jacob Thoman and Louisa Fernando, the film centers the quiet gestures that carry the memory of former lovers – between the way you stack the dishwasher, the cereal you eat, or how you brush your teeth. Moving from the intimate confines of their apartment to the streets of New York, tension builds across daily routines, culminating in a final moment of release that takes hold on the subway platform. Against a performance by cellist Coleman Itzcoff, the film’s emotional climax observes the emptiness attached to love and loss through the lens of mourning – as a feeling to be overcome, exorcized, and ultimately set free.
items.title: ​LoveSick: Chambers Street

items.text: ​Inner Worlds: Khushnu Panthaki Hoof “Always remember that every morning you’re born in the skin of a donkey.” This unexpected advice was passed by Le Corbusier to the Indian architect B.V. Doshi – a close associate of the modernist pioneer in 1950s Paris, and a leading figure whose architecture helped redefine modernism in India. For Doshi’s granddaughter, Khushnu Panthaki Hoof, the phrase has become a compass – a reminder to approach each day without attachment to past achievements. Based in Ahmedabad, India, Panthaki Hoof carries forward lessons from her grandfather at the practice he founded here, Studio Sangath – which she now leads together with her husband, architect Sönke Hoof. For this episode of Inner Worlds , Art Basel visits Studio Sangath as Panthaki Hoof continues the practice’s evolving architectural legacy, with a focus on carefully chosen materials, light, and texture to create spaces that foster a sense of human connection. This sensibility also shapes the Doshi Retreat at the Vitra Campus in Weil am Rhein, Germany – a contemplative pavilion completed in 2025 by Studio Sangath from one of B.V. Doshi’s final concepts. Just minutes from Basel, the structure offers visitors a rare opportunity to experience the architect’s only realized project outside India and the final major work of his career.
items.title: ​Inner Worlds: Khushnu Panthaki Hoof

items.text: ​World Service: In the Same Light For the short film In the Same Light , New York-based Ethiopian filmmaker Herrana Addisu illustrates the essential role immigrants play in U.S. society, recognizing their place not on the periphery, but at its center. Developed with Communities United for Status & Protection (CUSP) and its member organizations, In the Same Light centers the lives of Temporary Protected Status (TPS) holders across America – people displaced by armed conflict, climate disasters, or political instability, yet deeply rooted in the communities they now call home – capturing parents, artists, business owners, neighbors, and community leaders, and the lives shaped beyond the political narrative. - Herrana Addisu Between morning routines, work, family life, creative expression, and moments of quiet reflection, In the Same Light uses observational visual language to move beyond headlines and policy debates, challenging the notion of immigrant communities as "others". Through quiet acts of everyday life, the film affirms that belonging is lived through contribution, connection, and shared humanity, shifting the focus towards the lived experience of people working and building futures despite growing uncertainty around their legal status.
items.title: ​World Service: In the Same Light

items.text: In the 1960s, 70s, and 80s, movie theaters displayed photographs in their lobbies, offering audiences a glimpse of what awaited them beyond the box office. By contract, these materials were meant to be destroyed once a film’s run had ended. One of the most common ways to do so was to burn them in a vacant lot nearby. A cinephile projectionist in Colombia decided to keep them instead, and decades later an archive of nearly 10,000 of these images resurfaced in Medellín.Fuego Camina Conmigo is an edition of 300 numbered books, each one unique, made from that archive. Its pages are composed of original silver gelatin lobby cards and, on the reverse, risograph-printed frames from films in which fire appears. As the pages are turned, both images coexist for a brief moment and, for a fraction of a second, these photographs catch fire only in our eyes.The book also includes a short reflection by the publishers on the relationship between cinema and fire. Rather than producing an English-language version of the book, the publishers translated the text into a matchbook that accompanies the Spanish edition and includes a small cutout from one of the original lobby cards.For ICP’s Book on the Wall series, La Bruja Ediciones and Ediciones Réplica expanded the publication into an installation composed of the same materials found in the book: original movie posters, photographs, and risograph prints, accompanied by a projection of excerpts from fire scenes in the films. The installation takes the form of a modular wheat-pasted mural attached to one of the bookstore’s walls, bringing into focus the circulation, persistence, and afterlife of images. About the publishers Ediciones Réplica is an independent Colombian publishing collective and imprint founded by researchers and artists Arturo Salazar and José Ruiz. Its work focuses on the research, editing, and publication of photographic archives and printed culture from Latin America.Through photobooks, exhibitions, and curatorial projects, Ediciones Réplica gives new form to historical visual collections, bringing together archival research, popular graphic culture, and the material histories of photography.La Bruja Ediciones is an independent publishing studio and print workshop based in Medellín, Colombia, specializing in risograph printing. Run by Ximena Escobar and Andrew Smith they focused on artists’ books, zines, and small-run editions, the project combines manual printing techniques with carefully selected papers to produce numbered publications and graphic works rooted in material experimentation. About Book on the WallBooks are central to photography, as they are to our exhibitions, school and library here at ICP. Editing, sequencing, design and choice of materials are vital elements in the making of any photobook. For The Book on the Wall we invite publishers to give an insight into the making of a recent book through a one-wall feature in ICP's shop, which is free and open to the public seven days a week. SPANISHEn las décadas de 1960, 70 y 80, los cines mostraban fotografías en sus vestíbulos, ofreciendo al público un adelanto de lo que les esperaba al otro lado de la taquilla. Por contrato, estos materiales debían destruirse una vez finalizada la exhibición de la película. Una de las formas más habituales era quemarlos en algún lote baldío cercano. Un proyeccionista cinéfilo en Colombia decidió conservarlos y, décadas más tarde, un archivo de cerca de 10.000 de estas imágenes reapareció en Medellín.Fuego Camina Conmigo es una edición de 300 libros numerados, todos distintos entre sí, realizada a partir de ese archivo. Sus páginas están compuestas por fotografias originales impresas en gelatina de plata y, en el reverso, por fotogramas impresos en risografía de películas en las que aparece el fuego. Al pasar las páginas, ambas imágenes conviven por un instante y, durante una fracción de segundo, estas fotografías arden únicamente en nuestros ojos.El libro también incluye una breve reflexión de los editores sobre la relación entre cine y fuego. La traducción al inglés se presenta en forma de una caja de fósforos que acompaña la edición en español e incluye un pequeño recorte de una de las fotografias originales.Para la serie Book on the Wall del ICP, La Bruja Ediciones y Ediciones Réplica llevaron la publicación al espacio expositivo mediante una instalación. Un mural modular fijado con engrudo a una de las paredes de la librería, compuesto por los mismos materiales presentes en el libro, afiches originales de cine, fotografías e impresiones en risografía, se complementa con una proyección de fragmentos de escenas de fuego de las películas. En conjunto, la instalación dirige la mirada hacia la circulación y persistencia de las imágenes. Sobre los editoresEdiciones Réplica es un colectivo y sello editorial independiente colombiano fundado por los investigadores y artistas Arturo Salazar y José Ruiz. Su trabajo se centra en la investigación, edición y publicación de archivos fotográficos y cultura impresa de América Latina.A través de fotolibros, exposiciones y proyectos curatoriales, Ediciones Réplica da nuevas formas a colecciones visuales históricas y explora las relaciones entre los archivos, la gráfica popular y las historias materiales de la fotografía.La Bruja Ediciones es un taller y estudio de edición independiente con sede en Medellín, Colombia, especializado en impresión risográfica. Dirigido por Ximena Escobar y Andrew Smith, el proyecto produce libros de artista, fanzines y ediciones de pequeña escala, combinando técnicas de impresión manual con una cuidadosa selección de papeles para dar forma a publicaciones numeradas y obras gráficas marcadas por la experimentación material. Acerca de Book on the WallLos libros ocupan un lugar central en la fotografía, al igual que en nuestras exposiciones, nuestra escuela y nuestra biblioteca en ICP. La edición, la secuenciación, el diseño y la elección de los materiales son elementos fundamentales en la creación de cualquier fotolibro.Book on the Wall, ofrece a las editoriales la oportunidad de presentar el proceso creativo detrás de una publicación reciente a través de una instalación especial en una de las paredes de la tienda de ICP, un espacio gratuito y abierto al público los siete días de la semana.
items.title: Book on the Wall

items.text: ICP’s Incubator is located on ICP’s ground floor. The space is free and open to the public during café and museum hours. Photographer and ICP alum Andrea Hernández Briceño has been documenting the indigenous Huöttöja people in the Amazon basin since 2024. Many members of the Huöttöja community feel the pull of the mining industry, which promises to make them rich but threatens to dilute their culture. Hernández Briceño was invited to document their traditions and ancestral knowledge as a way to preserve them, but also set herself the goal to avoid being another outsider that left them feeling exploited. She aims to create a body of work that reflects the Huöttöja worldview and spirituality by nurturing a long term relationship with the community and its main goal: to show us that humans are not separate from nature, but an extension of it. About The ArtistAndrea Hernández Briceño is a Venezuelan journalist, visual storyteller, Magnum Foundation fellow, TEDx speaker and National Geographic explorer based in Caracas. She tells representative stories about gender, environment and social phenomena that put the dignity of participants in the center of the narrative while using different formats and a perceptive approach to magic. She was awarded the Women Photograph grant in 2024, three POY Latams (2023 and 2025) and has been shortlisted for the Marilyn Stafford Award twice. Hernández Briceño co-founded the all women collectives Ayün Fotógrafas and Solunar. She teaches the Ojo Pelao and MiraVzla free workshops, making photography education more accessible in her country. She believes in the importance of creating alliances with communities through horizontal practices that combine education, community building and storytelling initiatives. Her work explores narratives that search for humanity in the space between seemingly opposing ideas as a result of growing up, documenting and resisting in Venezuela, a place of deep contradictions. As part of a team effort she published a book called Mango Season that looks into food insecurity and the resolve to live a dignified life in Venezuela with Raya Editorial and El Archivo. Hernández Briceño collaborates with The Washington Post, The Guardian, El País and other international outlets. Her work has been exhibited in New York, Paris, Mexico City and Venezuela. About the ICP Incubator SpaceICP’s Incubator Space is a new exhibition program designed to highlight the work of emerging photographers who are responding in real time to the world around us. ICP will present a rotating selection of projects by imagemakers experimenting with and pushing boundaries around the documentary tradition. ICP’s Incubator Space is curated by Sara Ickow, Associate Director of Exhibitions at ICP. Header image: A light shines upon the tip of the Huöttöja communal home “churuata” in the Amazonas state, Venezuela, on April 21, 2025. In the indigenous Huöttöja people’s worldview, this branch functions as an antenna connecting the earth with the spiritual realm. The “churuata” is the place where shaman Jattupa tells the stories that conform the Huöttöja way of life, viewing humans not as separate from nature but as an extension of it.

items.text: I’m So Happy You Are Here: Japanese Women Photographers from the 1950s to Now reconsiders and expands on the history of Japanese photography through the work of more than twenty-five intergenerational artists from the 1950s to today. Presented in partnership with Aperture and curated by Pauline Vermare, Lesley A. Martin, and Takeuchi Mariko, the exhibition includes work Fujioka Aya, Hara Mikiko, Hiromix, Ishikawa Mao, Ishiuchi Miyako, Katayama Mari, Kawauchi Rinko, Komatsu Hiroko, Kon Michiko, Nagashima Yurie, Narahashi Asako, Ninagawa Mika, Nishimura Tamiko, Noguchi Rika, Nomura Sakiko, Okabe Momo, Okanoue Toshiko, Onodera Yuki, Sawada Tomoko, Shiga Lieko, Sugiura Kunié, Suzuki Nozomi, Tawada Yuki, Tokiwa Toyoko, Ushioda Tokuko, Watanabe Hitomi, Yamazawa Eiko and Yanagi Miwa.Spanning photography, installation, photobooks, and printed matter, the exhibition explores the experiences and perspectives of women in Japan through delicate and blunt observations of everyday life, critical reflections on the roles inhabited and reshaped by Japanese women, and approaches that push the boundaries of photography. The exhibition is accompanied by a major publication (Aperture, 2024) that expands upon content of the show through essays, historical material, and a comprehensive bibliography of photobooks made by Japanese women. About Curators:Lesley A. Martin is executive director of Printed Matter. Most recently, she was the creative director of Aperture, where she served as editor on more than one hundred books on photography, and as founding publisher of The PhotoBook Review. Takeuchi Mariko is a photography critic, curator, and professor at Kyoto University of the Arts. Previously, she served as visiting researcher at the National Museum of Modern Art, Tokyo, and at the National Museum of Art, Osaka. Pauline Vermare is the Phillip and Edith Leonian Curator, Photography, Brooklyn Museum. Previously, she served as the cultural director of Magnum Photos in New York, and curator at the International Center of Photography and the Museum of Modern Art, New York. About Aperture:Aperture is a nonprofit publisher that leads conversations around photography worldwide. From its base in New York, Aperture connects global audiences and supports artists through its acclaimed quarterly magazine, books, exhibitions, digital platforms, public programs, limited-edition prints, and awards. Established in 1952 to advance “creative thinking, significantly expressed in words and photographs,” Aperture champions photography’s vital role in nurturing curiosity and encouraging a more just, tolerant society. For more information, visit aperture.org Header image: Kawauchi Rinko, Untitled, 2004; from the series the eyes, the ears. Courtesy the artist and Aperture
items.title: I'm So Happy You Are Here: Japanese Women Photographers from the 1950s to Now

items.text: Photobooks USA 2000–25 explores how the photobook has emerged as a powerful tool for artists to respond to the forces shaping contemporary life in the United States. Over the past 25 years, this evolution is reflected in the growing number of publications, a wider range of publishers, bold experimentation in design, and greater inclusivity.Featuring over 50 photobooks—from out-of-print titles to more recent landmark publications—the exhibition presents a cross section of creators, themes, forms, and subjects, examining how artists have used the photobook to present and circulate their work in powerful and innovative ways. Rather than defining a canon or focusing on bestselling or award-winning titles, the exhibition brings together books that explore different aspects of the United States. Taken as a whole, they reflect the artistic, social, cultural, political, environmental and economic conversations shaping the twenty-first century.Curated by ICP’s Creative Director David Campany, Associate Director of Exhibitions Sara Ickow, and Curatorial Assistant malaika newsome."A paradise for those who want to take a glance at the photographic collections from the last 25 years of the United States.”—Musée Magazine Featured Photobooks +Farah Al Qasimi, Hello Future, 2022 (Capricious)Ana Andrade, Río de agua viva, 2023 (Taller California)Peter van Agtmael, Sorry for the War, 2020 (Mass Books)Chris Aluka Berry, Affrilachia: Testimonies, 2024 (University Press of Kentucky)Adriant Khadafhi Bereal, The Black Yearbook, 2024 (4 Color Books)Widline Cadet, Ritual [Dis]Appearance / Seremoni Disparisyon, 2025 (Black Mountain Studio)Anne Collier, Anne Collier, 2014 (Museum of Contemporary Art Chicago)Debi Cornwall, Necessary Fictions, 2020 (Radius Books)Philip-Lorca diCorcia, a Storybook Life, 2003 (Twin Palms Publishers)Jess T. items.text: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography explores the powerful relationship Yves Saint Laurent forged between fashion and photography over four decades, revealing how photography functioned not only as a tool for promotion but also as a creative force that helped define a legacy. Organized in collaboration with the Musée Yves Saint Laurent Paris and the Fondation Pierre Bergé–Yves Saint Laurent, it brings together nearly 300 iconic photographs and archival objects.Featuring work by celebrated artists such as Richard Avedon, Cecil Beaton, Guy Bourdin, Robert Doisneau, Horst P. Horst, William Klein, Annie Leibovitz, Steven Meisel, Duane Michals, Helmut Newton, Irving Penn, David Seidner, Andy Warhol, and others, the exhibition pairs these photographs with contact sheets, campaign materials, magazines, and personal images. Together, they reveal how Yves Saint Laurent established a blueprint for the way the fashion world is visualized and understood today.Curated by Simon Baker, Guest Curator and photography historian; Nastasia Alberti, Deputy Head of Collections and Head Archivist; and Clémentine Cuinet, Head of Photographic Collections, both of the Musée Yves Saint Laurent Paris."The show takes visitors into the expansive visual universe of a designer whose work continues to shape how fashion is perceived today."– W Magazine "The show left me fascinated. Craving for more." - Musée Magazine"To see his clothes through the eyes of photographers such as Helmut Newton, Richard Avedon, David Bailey and more, as you do in the ICP show, is to see the way fashion and photography came together to crystallize moments of change in gender relations, culture, and society in the second half of the 20th century."—The New York Times About Yves Saint LaurentYves Mathieu-Saint-Laurent (b. 1936, Oran, Algeria) began his career in Paris in 1955 as an assistant to renowned couturier Christian Dior. Following Dior’s sudden death in 1957, Yves Saint Laurent was appointed artistic director of the Musée Yves Saint Laurent Paris and his designs quickly gained critical acclaim. Dismissed from Dior in 1960 after military service and a subsequent hospitalization for depression, he founded his own couture house in partnership with Pierre Bergé, whom he had met in 1958. The first collection was presented in 1962 and the house became known for reinterpreting traditionally masculine garments like pantsuits and trench coats into bold, feminine looks that redefined the wardrobe of the modern woman. In 1966, he launched SAINT LAURENT rive gauche, a ready-to-wear line with stores opened internationally. Influenced by different cultures, Yves Saint Laurent’s designs paid tribute to artists including Mondrian, Picasso and Van Gogh and drew inspiration from faraway places––most notably Marrakech. This prolific output spanned thirty years and was the subject of numerous awards and exhibitions across the globe. Yves Saint Laurent retired and closed the couture house in 2002, at which point the newly-formed Fondation Pierre Bergé–Yves Saint Laurent assumed responsibility for advancing and safeguarding his work. Yves Saint Laurent died at his Paris home on June 1, 2008, at the age of seventy-one.In 2017, the Fondation Pierre Bergé–Yves Saint Laurent was re-envisioned with the opening of two museums in Paris and Marrakech. These institutions preserve and celebrate one of the twentieth century’s most defining creative legacies.About Musée Yves Saint Laurent ParisThe Musée Yves Saint Laurent Paris, chaired by Madison Cox, is the first museum dedicated to one of the greatest couturiers of the twentieth century to open in the capital of fashion. It opened its doors on October 3, 2017, fifteen years after the closing of the haute couture house and obtained the designation ‘Musée de France.’ It occupies the historic hôtel particulier at 5 Avenue Marceau where for nearly thirty years, from 1974 to 2002, Yves Saint Laurent’s creative genius held sway. The museum's vast collection includes 12,000 garments, including 8,300 haute couture pieces more than 100,000 graphic artworks, including 55,000 fashion sketches by the couturier and 194,000 photographs comprising of diapositives, negatives and vintage and modern prints, as well as an archive of more than 4,000 magazines and 60,000 documents, including correspondence, audiovisual materials and personal objects, completing the collection. This ensemble is the result of the pioneering and systematic archival work initiated by Yves Saint Laurent and Pierre Bergé. The Musée Yves Saint Laurent Paris is temporarily closed for renovations. It is scheduled to reopen in the fall of 2027. Header image: Ensemble worn by Edia Vairelli, Haute Couture Spring/Summer 1982 collection, 5 Avenue Marceau, Paris, January 1982. Polaroid by fashion house staff © All rights reserved © Yves Saint Laurent; Tailored suit worn by Anna Karin, Haute Couture Autumn/Winter 1991 collection, 5 Avenue Marceau, Paris, July 1991. items.text: If one thing is clear, film photography is not dead. We see it across genres, from commercial fashion photography to enthusiast communities. So, it’s perhaps not a coincidence that Negative Film Conversion has been one of our most requested features. Last year, we got to it. Now, let’s explore how the solution came to life, how it was designed, what needed to be right, and who we collaborated with to bring it to market. Designing the feature: Capture sets the foundation When scanning and converting film, the earliest decisions in the workflow have a lasting impact. That means the quality of the conversion depends heavily on how the negative is captured. To get the best starting point, the goal is to extract as much information as possible from the film, which means maximizing dynamic range and minimizing digital noise during scanning. A camera-based scanning setup with a stable film carrier helps keep the negative sharp, while an even, consistent light source ensures uniform exposure across the frame. When the camera is tethered to Capture One, exposure warnings make it easy to immediately see whether a capture is over- or underexposed, … The post Behind the feature: Negative Film Conversion in Capture One appeared first on Capture One .

items.text: Tom Barreto is a Brazilian photographer and director whose work balances technical precision with emotional sensitivity. Born in Taubaté and now based in São Paulo, he has built a career spanning fashion, beauty, advertising, and personal projects, creating images defined by choreographed light, atmosphere, and a deep sense of humanity. His portfolio includes campaigns for Dior Beauty, Fendi, Arezzo, and Bvlgari, alongside collaborations with artists such as IZA, Fernanda Torres, and Marina Ruy Barbosa. His work has appeared in global publications including Glamour, Harper’s Bazaar, Marie Claire, and Numéro Netherlands. Tom’s practice goes beyond creating imagery. At its core is a philosophy rooted in observation: of people, of everyday life, and of the quiet tensions that exist beneath the surface. It’s this mindset that shapes how he approaches storytelling, navigates uncertainty, and maintains a consistent visual language across analog and digital workflows. In our chat below, Tom reflects on why observation is a photographer’s most valuable material, how he trains his eye away from the camera, and what keeps him grounded when the industry feels unstable. He also shares insights into his creative process, the role of controlled chaos in his work, and how … The post Why observation is a photographer’s greatest material appeared first on Capture One .

items.text: AI needs no introduction. It’s wildly powerful. It ramped quickly and flooded every corner of our lives, and yes, a few big tech companies behaved like hooligans along the way, which naturally created fear, dread, and the sense that something precious was slipping away. But here’s the hard truth: AI has already been adopted. Every single photographer we’ve spoken to in the past six months, and that’s hundreds of them, is using it. Not as a philosophical debate. Not as an existential threat. Just as a tool to run their business. To build mood boards. To finish images. To keep up. AI is reshaping photography at a pace we haven’t seen since the transition from film to digital. And this is uncomfortable. Profoundly uncomfortable. But if there’s anything we’ve learned from Patrick Fore, we need to “sit with the discomfort.” So now the real question is: What future are we building toward? Back to the Metal When everything gets louder, the best photographers do something counterintuitive: They go quieter. They slow down. They find community. They create with more intention and honesty. Because the world doesn’t need less photography. It needs more. More authorship, more … The post Seriously. AI is not the main character. appeared first on Capture One .
items.title: Seriously. AI is not the main character.

items.text: Fashion and beauty photographer Sarah Silver might be best known for her bold, playful, and colorful visuals, but behind every image lies a bulletproof workflow and process that make it all possible. For Sarah, creativity doesn’t come from chaos. It’s built on structure. In this quick Q&A, she shares how preparation fuels her imagination, the systems that keep her shoots running smoothly, and the tools she relies on from concept to final export. What’s essential when prepping for a shoot? There’s so much information coming at me from all sides while prepping a shoot, so filing everything properly for easy retrieval is a MUST. I have pre-made digital folders, shoot sessions, job checklists/tasks, and naming conventions that keep me on track and consistent for EVERY project I undertake. I find that if all the pieces are in the right place, it opens my mind to maximum creativity. Do you have any post-production advice for us? Post means so much more to me than just retouching. It also includes proper file storage. When backing up files after a shoot, I use on-site RAIDS … The post How Sarah Silver stays creative by being organized appeared first on Capture One .

items.text: We followed art director Olivia Cho Lund as she captured her latest project, Finding You to Find Myself, a striking blend of surreal portraiture and deeply personal storytelling. We got the chance to see her own layered identity, and the collaborative energy of her team helped shape the project. Born and raised in Brazil, living in France, and now based in Denmark, Olivia has always carried a complex sense of self. But it was her Korean heritage, the “deepest hidden layer,” as she calls it, that inspired this journey. Along the way, she connected with Danish individuals who were subjected to adoption from Korea, people who, like her, navigate the intertwined paths of belonging and self-definition. “This project is an exploration of cultural identity, a red thread that weaves together immigrants, multicultural individuals, and those subjected to adoption, revealing one of the many facets that shape who we are: our Koreanness.” From makeup dusted with gochugaru, to gestures frozen mid-movement, to surreal camera effects revealing inner worlds, every detail was intentional. items.text: Sidus Link Pro 2.4 introduces Action Panel, a dedicated full-screen view that turns your cues and Quickshots into big, customizable “Go” buttons for fast, confident execution. Assign your cues to buttons, choose their size, and fire them at exactly the right moment. With an optional passcode lock, Action Panel makes it easy to hand control to a client, operator, or venue team. Action Panel is available in the Pro plan for $10/month, with MAX adding support for 4 universes of control. Plus, Sidus Link Pro is now available on Mac!
